_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Barnes County, ND. Transition county between lake plain and drift prairie. County seat at Valley City on the Sheyenne River. Record survival is good from county organization (1875). Valley City Normal School records supplement county records.
display_name: Barnes County, North Dakota — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Valley City — wooden frame building, temporary quarters
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as population grew after the Northern Pacific arrived.
Replaced by the current 1901 building. Records transferred without loss.

records_destroyed: some scattered early justice of the peace records
record_losses.description: Barnes County has experienced no major courthouse fire, flood, or other catastrophic event affecting its records. The Sheyenne River occasionally flooded the valley bottom at Valley City, but the courthouse on the valley rim was never threatened. Record survival is good from county organization in 1875.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Barnes County's records are well-preserved. If {ancestor_name} left a trace in Barnes County after 1875, the record is almost certainly still in Valley City.
record_losses.source: State Historical Society of North Dakota. Guide to County Records. Bismarck: SHSND, 2003.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county organization. Northern Pacific land-grant deeds prominent in the earliest records.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Federal homestead patents at BLM GLO.
deeds.repository: Barnes County Recorder's Office, Valley City; SHSND

wills_estates.repository: Barnes County Clerk of Court; SHSND

marriages.completeness: Good from county organization. Norwegian Lutheran church marriages sometimes preceded civil licensing.
marriages.gaps: Church marriages without civil license in the earliest settlement period.
marriages.repository: Barnes County Recorder's Office, Valley City

court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in very early territorial proceedings.
court_minutes.repository: Barnes County Clerk of Court; SHSND

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1910. Valley City town records may be more complete than rural records for 1893-1910.
vital_records.repository: ND Department of Health Vital Records; SHSND

substitute_sources.description: Norwegian Lutheran congregations throughout Barnes County maintained ministerialbok records — baptisms, confirmations, marriages, burials — in Norwegian.
substitute_sources.repository: NAHA, Northfield, MN; ELCA Archives; individual congregations; SHSND
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Norwegian immigrant families. The only record of vital events before 1893 in many rural communities.
substitute_sources.source: Lovoll, Odd S. The Promise of America. Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 1984.

substitute_sources.description: The Valley City Normal School (est. 1890, later Valley City State University) maintained enrollment records, student directories, and yearbooks. Many Barnes County farm families sent daughters to the Normal School to become teachers.
substitute_sources.repository: Valley City State University Archives, Valley City
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Enrollment records include hometown, parents' names (in early years), and sometimes a photograph. Useful for tracking second-generation immigrant families transitioning from farming to professional work.
substitute_sources.source: Robinson, Elwyn B. History of North Dakota. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1966.

substitute_sources.description: Homestead application files with detailed personal information.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for settlers. Barnes County homestead files document both the flat lake plain in the east and the harder drift prairie in the west — the two landscapes that divided the county's agricultural experience.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Homestead Records.

narrative_hooks: Barnes County's records are intact in Valley City — no fire, no flood ever reached the courthouse on the valley rim, even when the Sheyenne rose to fill the gorge below.

If {ancestor_name}'s daughter attended the Valley City Normal School, the enrollment records may contain {pronoun_possessive} hometown, parents' names, and a photograph — the paper trail left when a farm family's daughter became a teacher.

Before 1893, the Norwegian Lutheran ministerialbok may be the only record that {ancestor_name} was born at all — the pastor's entry in Norwegian, recording the baptism in a county where the state did not yet require anyone to register a birth.

{ancestor_name}'s homestead in Barnes County sat at the boundary between two landscapes — the flat lake plain where the soil ran six feet deep and the drift prairie where glacial boulders had to be hauled from the field before the plow could turn a furrow.
source_references: State Historical Society of North Dakota. Guide to County Records. Bismarck: SHSND, 2003.
Robinson, Elwyn B. History of North Dakota. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1966.
Lovoll, Odd S. The Promise of America: A History of the Norwegian-American People. Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 1984.
National Archives and Records Administration. Homestead Records at the National Archives. Washington, DC.
